claims.claim: When inference-time memory pressure requires reducing per-token runtime state, one strategy is to skip entire transformer layers.

evidence_refs.quote: # Layer Skip under Memory Pressure: Calibrated Layer Selection Outperforms Static Skip Policies in a Residual-Stack Simulation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We investigate whether a calibrated policy—skipping layers with the lowest observed residual-delta norm during a brief calibration phase—preserves full-model output better than static heuristics (skip-tail, skip-front, uniform, random) at matched skip counts.

evidence_refs.quote: # Layer Skip under Memory Pressure: Calibrated Layer Selection Outperforms Static Skip Policies in a Residual-Stack Simulation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: In a dependency-light NumPy simulation of a 24-layer residual MLP stack across four random seeds and four pressure levels (12.5%–50% layers skipped), the calibrated policy achieved the lowest relative mean squared error in all 16 of 16 comparisons.

evidence_refs.quote: # Layer Skip under Memory Pressure: Calibrated Layer Selection Outperforms Static Skip Policies in a Residual-Stack Simulation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: At 12.5% skip fraction, calibrated relative MSE was 0.000204 versus 0.00378 for the best baseline (a 14.3× improvement ratio); at 50% skip fraction, calibrated relative MSE was 0.00953 versus 0.0239 (a 2.5× improvement ratio).

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Class-agreement proxies remained above 0.91 even at 50% skip fraction.

claims.claim: Inference on large language models under memory-constrained conditions—such as edge devices with no swap, or shared servers approaching `MemAvailable` thresholds—creates pressure to reduce per-token runtime state.

evidence_refs.quote: # Layer Skip under Memory Pressure: Calibrated Layer Selection Outperforms Static Skip Policies in a Residual-Stack Simulation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Each layer applies a fixed random linear transformation followed by ReLU, composed via residual connections: $$\mathbf{x}_{l+1} = \mathbf{x}_l + \text{ReLU}(\mathbf{W}_l \mathbf{x}_l + \mathbf{b}_l)$$ Weights $\mathbf{W}_l \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ and biases $\mathbf{b}_l \in \mathbb{R}^d$ are drawn once per seed and frozen.

evidence_refs.quote: # Layer Skip under Memory Pressure: Calibrated Layer Selection Outperforms Static Skip Policies in a Residual-Stack Simulation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: For each layer $l$, we compute the residual-delta norm: $$\delta_l = \|\mathbf{x}_{l+1} - \mathbf{x}_l\|_2$$ averaged over calibration samples.

evidence_refs.quote: # Layer Skip under Memory Pressure: Calibrated Layer Selection Outperforms Static Skip Policies in a Residual-Stack Simulation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Relative MSE**: $\text{MSE}(\mathbf{y}_{\text{skipped}}, \mathbf{y}_{\text{full}}) / \text{MSE}(\mathbf{y}_{\text{full}}, \mathbf{0})$, where $\mathbf{y}$ is the final stack output.
